title: CO Iso-Raise vs 3 Limpers — KJo
prompt: $2/$5 live. UTG, UTG+1, and HJ all limp. You are CO with K♥J♦ (100bb). Iso-raise, limp behind, or fold?

options.label: Fold
Limp Behind
Iso-Raise

answer.explanation: KJo is a strong iso-raise candidate from CO vs passive live limpers. Size to 5-6x (25-30) to get heads-up IP. Limping behind concedes initiative and inflates a multiway pot with a hand that plays better HU.

A.explanation: Pool A may flat or 3-bet iso-raises, but KJo is still strong enough to iso from CO. Size up to deny squeeze callers.

B.explanation: Pool B passive limpers fold to ISO or call one at a time. Get HU with KJo IP — clear iso-raise.

C.explanation: Pool C aggro players may squeeze, but KJo still iso-raises profitably from CO. Be prepared for a squeeze and have a plan.

common_mistakes: Limping behind with strong hands, forfeiting initiative
Under-sizing iso-raise and getting multiple callers
coaching_context.population_note: Live limpers often fold to large iso-raises or call with dominated hands — both outcomes are profitable for KJo.
coaching_context.key_concept: KJo is a strong iso-raise candidate from CO vs passive live limpers.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: CO Iso-Raise vs 3 Limpers — KJo is difficult because many players default to limping behind with strong hands, forfeiting initiative, even though KJo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: KJo iso-raises to 5-6x from CO to get heads-up in position — limping behind inflates a multiway pot with a hand that dominates passive limpers but plays poorly against multiple callers.

title: BTN Iso-Raise vs 2 Limpers — A9o
prompt: $2/$5 live. MP and CO limp. You are BTN with A♣9♦ (100bb). Iso-raise to 4-5x or limp?

options.label: Fold
Limp Behind
Iso-Raise

answer.explanation: A9o on BTN vs 2 passive limpers is a textbook iso-raise. You have position, a top-pair-capable hand, and dominated limpers. Raise to 4-5x to get heads-up.

A.explanation: Competent players may 3-bet iso-raises from the blinds — A9o has good enough equity to continue. Still iso.

B.explanation: Passive recs limp-call dominated hands constantly. Iso-raise A9o for value IP — this is free money.

C.explanation: Even aggro pools iso-raise A9o profitably from BTN. Their 3-bets don't dominate A9o often enough to deter.

common_mistakes: Limping A9o behind and surrendering positional edge
Over-folding in fear of 3-bet squeezes
coaching_context.population_note: A9o crushes passive limper ranges. Iso-raise is mandatory — every limp-behind costs expected value.
coaching_context.key_concept: A9o on BTN vs 2 passive limpers is a textbook iso-raise.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Iso-Raise works because A9o is either too dominated or too awkward on later streets to justify continuing more aggressively.

title: BTN Facing 4 Limpers — 87s, Iso or Limp?
prompt: $2/$5 live. UTG, UTG+1, HJ, and CO all limp. You are BTN with 8♠7♠ (100bb). Iso-raise to 7x or limp behind?

options.label: Fold
Limp Behind
Iso-Raise to 7x

answer.explanation: With 4 limpers, iso-raising 87s bloats the pot multiway even vs a 7x size. 87s is a speculative suited connector that thrives with implied odds and position. Limp behind, see a cheap multiway flop, and extract big when you hit.

A.explanation: Competent players recognize 4-limper spots favor limping behind with speculative hands. Iso-raise inflates pot vs too many opponents.

B.explanation: Passive limpers rarely fold to even large iso-raises with 4 players in. Limp, get your implied odds, and flop big.

C.explanation: Aggro pool in the blinds can squeeze a 7x iso-raise, putting 87s in an awful spot. Limp behind and avoid the squeeze.

common_mistakes: Iso-raising every limped pot regardless of how many limpers
Sizing too small vs 4 limpers and getting called by everyone
coaching_context.population_note: The iso-raise threshold tightens sharply with 4+ limpers. Suited connectors prefer multiway implied-odds spots over being the isolation aggressor.
coaching_context.key_concept: With 4 limpers, iso-raising 87s bloats the pot multiway even vs a 7x size.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: BTN Facing 4 Limpers — 87s, Iso or Limp? is difficult because many players default to iso-raising every limped pot regardless of how many limpers, even though 87s changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Limp Behind works because 87s makes its money by realizing implied odds and getting paid when it connects cleanly, not by forcing a bigger pot now.

title: Iso Pot IP — Blank Turn After Checking Back Dry Flop
prompt: BTN iso-raised 3 limpers to $30. Only CO called. Flop K♦7♣2♠ — BTN checks back. Turn: 4♦ (blank). CO checks. You have K♣J♠. Fire the delayed c-bet?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (~1/3 pot)
Bet Big (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: Classic delayed c-bet with TPTK on a dry board. CO checked twice — their range is capped to weak pairs and floats. Fire 2/3 pot: extract value from Kx-worse, 7x, and deny any backdoor equity.

A.explanation: Competent players may have slow-played Kx or 77/22 — use medium sizing (1/2 pot) to extract value without over-building the pot with TPTK.

B.explanation: Passive recs check-call with Kx-worse, 7x, even naked gutshots. Bet 2/3 pot for maximum value — their range is capped and sticky.

C.explanation: Aggro pool players also show down weak holdings after two checks. Bet big and fold out their air-heavy range.

common_mistakes: Checking back the turn again and giving free cards
Betting too small and allowing floats to realize equity cheaply
coaching_context.population_note: Two checks from a passive rec signals weakness. The delayed c-bet is the primary value extraction tool in iso pots — don't sleep through it.
coaching_context.key_concept: Classic delayed c-bet with TPTK on a dry board. A shifts to bet small (~1/3 pot) when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Iso Pot IP — Blank Turn After Checking Back Dry Flop is difficult because many players default to checking back the turn again and giving free cards, even though KJo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Bet Big (~2/3 pot) works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: Iso Pot IP — Flush Completes on Turn After Checking Flop
prompt: BTN iso-raised to $25. CO called. Flop J♥6♣2♠ — BTN checked back with K♠Q♠ (nut flush draw). Turn: 4♠ (flush completes). CO checks. Hero made the nut flush. Bet?

Bet Small (~1/3 pot)
Bet Large (~2/3 pot or more)

answer.explanation: Hero has the nut flush and CO checked. Bet large — CO's range is capped to Jx, 6x, and occasional floats. The nut flush is a building hand; extract maximum value now before the river might kill action.

A.explanation: Competent players may hold Ax♠ as a float. Medium sizing invites action and sets up river shove.

B.explanation: Passive recs never fold Jx or Ax facing a turn bet. Bet 2/3-3/4 pot and set up a river value bet.

C.explanation: Gambly pool players call off stack with top pair. Build pot aggressively with nut flush.

Betting too small and allowing river to kill action
coaching_context.population_note: Nut-flush on turn is a value emergency — build now. Live recs pay off top pair vs flush-completing boards far too often.

title: Iso Pot IP — Turn Pairs the Top Card, Hits Villain Range
prompt: BTN iso-raised to $30. CO called. Flop T♠7♣3♦ — checked through. Turn T♥ (top card pairs; hits CO's Tx range). CO checks. Hero has A♦Q♥ (no pair, no draw). Stab or check?

Bet Small (~1/3 pot)
Barrel (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: The paired T improves CO's limp-calling range (Tx trips, Tx two-pair). Hero has AQ with no pair, no draw — no equity, no fold equity. Checking back preserves showdown value and avoids a check-raise bluff-catch situation.

A.explanation: Competent player may check-raise the turn with trips. AQ has reverse implied odds — check and reassess river.

B.explanation: Passive recs call down with Tx on paired boards. Bluffing has no merit — check back AQ and take showdown when river blanks.

C.explanation: Aggro pool may check-raise the T — AQ can't continue. Check back and give up unless the river provides equity.

Bluffing into a board that dramatically improves villain's range

coaching_context.key_concept: The paired T improves CO's limp-calling range (Tx trips, Tx two-pair).

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check Back works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: Delayed C-Bet IP — Nut Flush Draw Checks Flop, Flush Hits Turn
prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop J♠7♠2♦ — BTN checked back with K♠Q♠ (nut flush draw). Turn 4♠ (flush completes). BB checks. Hero now has the nut flush. Bet?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Medium (~1/2 pot)
Bet Large (~3/4 pot)

answer.explanation: Hero flopped the nut flush draw and made it on the turn. BB checked again — they may have Jx, Ax♠ floats, or two-pair. Build the pot now with a 3/4-pot bet to set up a river shove. Don't let the hand play small.

A.explanation: Competent BB may have Ax♠ and call a big turn bet. Medium sizing is fine — sets up river value.

B.explanation: Passive BB calls off with Jx, Ax♠, even naked pairs on flush boards. Bet large and set up river extraction.

C.explanation: Aggro pool calls nutted hands or check-raises smaller — bet large to commit stacks with the best hand.

common_mistakes: Slowplaying the nut flush after checking back a draw on the flop
Betting too small and missing stack-building opportunity
coaching_context.population_note: You have the nuts and position — now is the time to build. The delayed c-bet with a nutted hand is a stack-building opportunity, not a trap setup.
coaching_context.key_concept: Hero flopped the nut flush draw and made it on the turn. A shifts to bet medium (~1/2 pot) when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Delayed C-Bet IP — Nut Flush Draw Checks Flop, Flush Hits Turn is difficult because many players default to slowplaying the nut flush after checking back a draw on the flop, even though KQs changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Hero made the nut flush on the turn and BB checked again — a 3/4-pot bet now sets up a river shove and extracts from Jx, Ax♠ floats, and two-pair hands that will not fold on this card.

title: Delayed C-Bet IP — Turn Pairs Top Card, Hits BB Range
prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop T♠7♦3♣ — checked through. Turn T♥ (pairs the top card). BB checks. Hero (BTN) has A♦Q♥ (no pair, no draw). Bluff or check?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (bluff)
Barrel Big (bluff)

answer.explanation: The T pairing the board significantly improves BB's BB defending range (Tx combos become trips). AQ has no equity and no fold equity against a range that has improved. Check back and take a free river — AQ has showdown value vs a complete bluff by the river.

A.explanation: Competent BB may check-raise the T with trips. AQ has zero equity after that — check back.

B.explanation: Passive BB calls down with Tx — bluffing has no merit. Check back and give up gracefully.

C.explanation: Aggro BB check-raises the turn with Tx for value. AQ can't continue — check and fold to a lead.

common_mistakes: Firing the delayed c-bet mechanically on any two-check sequence
Bluffing into boards that improved the calling range dramatically
coaching_context.population_note: Delayed c-bets require range awareness. A paired top card on the turn is a red flag — BB's range strengthened, not weakened. Check back.
coaching_context.key_concept: The T pairing the board significantly improves BB's BB defending range (Tx combos become trips).
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Delayed C-Bet IP — Turn Pairs Top Card, Hits BB Range is difficult because many players default to firing the delayed c-bet mechanically on any two-check sequence, even though AQo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check Back works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: OOP Turn Probe — BTN Checks Flop, Blank Turn, TP Hero Leads
prompt: BB called BTN open. Flop Q♣8♠4♦ — BTN checks back. Turn 2♣ (blank). Hero (BB) has Q♥T♦ (top pair decent kicker). Check or lead out?

options.label: Check
Lead Small (~1/3 pot)
Lead Large (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: BTN checked back a Q-high flop — their range is capped to weak holdings, missed overs, or occasional slow-plays. Leading small with TPTK is a profitable probe to deny free cards and extract value. Large lead or check are also reasonable depending on pool read.

A.explanation: Competent BTN may have slow-played Qx or flopped a set. Checking with TPTK gives you information — lead is a thin line.

B.explanation: Passive BTN rarely raises when they're beat. Small lead extracts value from 8x, 4x, and air floats.

C.explanation: Aggro BTN checks back range-wide sometimes. Small lead charges their equity — large lead risks fold from hands you beat.

common_mistakes: Checking top pair again after BTN shows weakness
Leading too large and folding hands you beat
coaching_context.population_note: OOP probes are underutilized in live cash. When the BTN checks back a board that favors them, they've shown weakness — small leads exploit passive BTNs profitably.
coaching_context.key_concept: BTN checked back a Q-high flop — their range is capped to weak holdings, missed overs, or occasional slow-plays. A shifts to check when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: OOP Turn Probe — BTN Checks Flop, Blank Turn, TP Hero Leads is difficult because many players default to checking top pair again after BTN shows weakness, even though QTo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Lead Small (~1/3 pot) works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: OOP Turn Probe — BTN Checks Flop, Air, Check or Bluff?
prompt: BB called BTN open. Flop A♠9♦6♣ — BTN checks back. Turn 2♥ (blank). Hero (BB) has K♣T♣ (no pair, no draw). Check or probe-bluff?

options.label: Check
Probe Small (bluff)
Probe Large (bluff)

answer.explanation: Hero has no equity and no draw on an ace-high board. Bluffing OOP into a BTN range that often includes Ax (checked back for deception or on a dry flop) is poor bluff selection. Check and give up — take the free river, fold to any bet.

A.explanation: Competent BTN checks back Ax often — bluffing KTo into an A-high board is low fold equity. Check.

B.explanation: Passive BTN calls down with pairs — no fold equity with KTo. Check and take free river.

C.explanation: Aggro BTN may check-raise or call and barrel — probing with no equity is losing in both scenarios.

common_mistakes: Probe-bluffing with no equity or fold equity, burning chips
Confusing BTN check-back (showing weakness) with a mandate to bluff
coaching_context.population_note: OOP bluffing requires fold equity. On an A-high board where BTN might have checked back Ax, KTo has none. Check and preserve stack.
coaching_context.key_concept: Hero has no equity and no draw on an ace-high board.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: OOP Turn Probe — BTN Checks Flop, Air, Check or Bluff? is difficult because many players default to probe-bluffing with no equity or fold equity, burning chips, even though KTs changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check works because KTs lands on the right side of the value-versus-bluff threshold for the exact line described in the drill.

title: OOP Turn Lead — BTN Checks Flop, Hero Hits Nut Straight on Turn
prompt: BB called BTN open. Flop J♣T♥5♦ — BTN checks back. Turn Q♠ (hero K♥9♦ makes nut straight). Hero leads or checks?

Lead Medium (~1/2 pot)
Lead Large (~2/3-3/4 pot)

answer.explanation: Hero has the nut straight OOP. The turn Q♠ gives BTN many connected hands (KJ, AK) that will call a large lead. Build the pot now — a big OOP lead with the nuts is correct. Slowplaying risks a blank river killing action.

B.explanation: Passive BTN calls off with KJx, two-pair, sets — lead large and extract maximum from their sticky range.

C.explanation: Aggro BTN may raise the lead — that's fine, you have the nuts. Lead large and stack off.

Leading too small and underbuilding with a strong hand
coaching_context.population_note: OOP value leads are underutilized. When BTN shows weakness and hero has the nuts on a connected board, lead big — BTN's J-T-Q connected range pays off.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: OOP Turn Lead — BTN Checks Flop, Hero Hits Nut Straight on Turn is difficult because many players default to slowplaying the nut straight OOP and losing action on a blank river, even though K9o changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Hero has the nut straight OOP and the turn Q♠ gives BTN connected hands (KJ, AK) that will call a large lead — slowplaying risks a blank river killing all action when the hand is at peak value now.

title: River Thin Value — Second Pair on Dry Board vs Passive Rec
prompt: SRP, IP. Board K♥7♣2♦4♠3♣ (very dry). Villain (passive rec) checks river. Hero has 7♦6♦ (second pair). Check back or bet for thin value?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (~1/3 pot)
Bet Large (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: Second pair on a K-7-2-4-3 dry board is thin but viable vs a passive rec. They called flop and checked twice — their range includes 7x-worse, 2x, and busted straight draws. Small bet extracts from the bottom of their range. Check is also reasonable.

A.explanation: Competent player's river check range includes hands that beat second pair. Check back — too thin.

B.explanation: Passive recs call river with pairs, 6x, busted draws. Small bet at 1/3 pot extracts from the bottom of their range.

C.explanation: Aggro pool checks down more often on blank rivers — second pair small bet is a fine exploit.

common_mistakes: Betting second pair too large and getting called by better hands
Always checking back thin hands and leaving value on the table
coaching_context.population_note: Live recs call river with a wide range of made hands. Second pair IP vs a passive player who checked twice is a small-bet spot — not a check-back by default.
coaching_context.key_concept: Second pair on a K-7-2-4-3 dry board is thin but viable vs a passive rec. A shifts to check back when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: River Thin Value — Second Pair on Dry Board vs Passive Rec is difficult because many players default to betting second pair too large and getting called by better hands, even though 76s changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: A small probe targets villain's 7x-worse, 2x, and busted draw holdings after they called flop and checked twice — larger sizing only gets called by hands that beat second pair.

title: River Sizing vs Sticky Pool — Strong Hand, Go Big
prompt: SRP, IP. Board Q♠J♦9♣3♥2♠ (dry runout). Villain checks river. Hero has Q♥Q♣ (set of queens). What sizing?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Medium (~1/2 pot)
Bet Large (~pot or overbet)

answer.explanation: Hero has top set on a dry runout. Villain called two streets — their range includes QJ, JJ, 9x, and is sticky. Bet large or overbet: live recs cannot fold two pair or overpairs to large river bets. Maximize extraction.

A.explanation: Competent player may have QJ, sets — medium sizing keeps their bluff-catching range in. Don't overbet a wet flop runout.

B.explanation: Passive rec two-street callers don't fold QJ or JJ to river bets. Bet pot-sized or overbet — they'll call.

C.explanation: Gambly pool calls off with top pair on river regularly. Overbet and extract max vs their under-folding tendency.

common_mistakes: Using medium sizing with nutted hands and leaving value on the table
Being too conservative vs sticky pools that don't fold top pair
coaching_context.population_note: Sticky live pools calling two streets on a connected board signal an inability to fold. Size up with strong hands — the calling frequency doesn't drop enough to justify going small.
coaching_context.key_concept: Hero has top set on a dry runout. A shifts to bet medium (~1/2 pot) when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.

title: Multiway Turn — Overpair on Low Board, Protection Bet or Check?
prompt: 3-way pot (BTN opened, MP and BB called). Pot $75. Board 7♠4♦2♣9♠ (turn). MP checks, BB checks. Hero (BTN) has A♣A♠. What to do?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (~1/4-1/3 pot)
Bet Large (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: Overpair in what became HU after BB folded — small protection bet is correct. Turn 9♠ adds a flush draw and gives MP potential two-pair with 97. Bet small (1/3 pot) to deny equity and extract thin value; large sizing bloats the pot vs sets and two-pair.

A.explanation: Competent player calls flop with sets, two-pair, draws. Aces face tough reverse implied odds — check and control pot.

B.explanation: Passive MP often floats flop with pairs or draws. Small bet charges them and extracts thin value.

C.explanation: Gambly MP may raise small bet with two pair — that's information you can use. Bet small, fold to raise.

common_mistakes: Betting large with overpairs in multiway pots
Checking back completely and giving free equity to draws
coaching_context.population_note: Multiway overpairs demand sizing discipline. Small protection bets serve dual purpose: extract value from worse pairs and charge draws. Large bets pot-commit you vs hands that have you crushed.
coaching_context.key_concept: Overpair in what became HU after BB folded — small protection bet is correct. A shifts to check back when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Multiway Turn — Overpair on Low Board, Protection Bet or Check? is difficult because many players default to betting large with overpairs in multiway pots, even though AA changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.

title: Multiway Turn — TPTK + Gutshot vs MP Donk Bet
prompt: 3-way pot (BTN/MP/BB). Pot $90. Board J♥9♣6♦5♦ (turn, connected). MP donk-leads $40. BB folds. Hero (BTN) has J♠T♦ (TPTK + gutshot). Call, fold, or raise?

options.label: Fold
Call
Raise

answer.explanation: TPTK + gutshot (8 outs to straight) has too much equity to fold vs a small MP donk. Call and see river. Raising is too thin multiway — MP has a wide donk range but could have 78s, 97s, sets that dominate. Call and realize equity.

A.explanation: Competent donk range includes sets/two-pair and draws — raising TPTK is too loose. Call with equity.

B.explanation: Passive MP donk range is value-heavy (JX, two-pair, draws). TPTK + gutshot has enough equity to call the small size.

C.explanation: Aggro MP may donk wide including bluffs. Call or small raise to define — don't over-commit.

common_mistakes: Folding TPTK + gutshot to a small multiway donk bet
Over-raising multiway without the top of your range
coaching_context.population_note: Multiway donk bets are frequently value-heavy in live pools. TPTK + straight draw has too much equity to fold small sizing — call and reassess river.
coaching_context.key_concept: TPTK + gutshot (8 outs to straight) has too much equity to fold vs a small MP donk.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: TPTK + 8 outs to the straight has too much equity to fold against a small multiway donk — raising is too thin with sets and two-pair in villain's donk range, so calling and realizing equity on the river is correct.

title: Multiway Turn — Semi-Bluff or Check on Dangerous Board?
prompt: 3-way pot (BTN/HJ/BB). Pot $65. Board Q♠J♦7♦T♠ (dangerous for aggressor). HJ and BB both check. Hero (BTN) has 8♠9♠ (OESD, no pair). Semi-bluff or check?

options.label: Check Back
Semi-Bluff Small
Semi-Bluff Large

answer.explanation: Q-J-7-T is an extremely connected board. Two opponents in multiway shrink fold equity to near zero — live players never fold two pair, sets, or straights. Semi-bluffing an OESD here has no merit. Check back and take the free river.

A.explanation: Competent players check connected boards with strong holdings. Semi-bluffing vs 2 opponents on Q-J-7-T has near-zero fold equity.

B.explanation: Passive recs call down with any pair or draw. Zero fold equity with OESD vs 2 players — check.

C.explanation: Aggro pool may check-raise semi-bluffs — putting 89s in a terrible spot. Check back.

common_mistakes: Semi-bluffing draws into multiway pots with highly connected boards
Confusing HU fold equity logic with multiway realities
coaching_context.population_note: Multiway semi-bluffing rules: fold equity drops sharply with 2+ opponents, especially on connected boards where calling ranges are strong. Take free cards — don't bluff.
coaching_context.key_concept: Q-J-7-T is an extremely connected board.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Multiway Turn — Semi-Bluff or Check on Dangerous Board? is difficult because many players default to semi-bluffing draws into multiway pots with highly connected boards, even though 89s changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check Back works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: Multiway River — Second Pair, Check or Thin Value?
prompt: 3-way pot to river (HJ/CO/BTN). Pot $110. Board A♠T♣5♦3♥2♠. HJ checks, CO checks. Hero (BTN) has T♦9♦ (second pair). Thin value bet or check?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (~1/4 pot)
Bet Large (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: Second pair on an A-high board in a 3-way pot is far too thin to bet. Both opponents checked two streets — their range still includes Ax, which beats second pair. Check back and take a free showdown. Betting risks being called by better and fold equity is minimal.

A.explanation: Multiway river with second pair is a clear check. At least one opponent likely has Ax.

B.explanation: Passive recs check Ax for pot control. Second pair calling range beats hero too often to bet.

C.explanation: Gambly players also check strong hands — check back second pair and protect showdown value.

common_mistakes: Thin-value betting in multiway river pots with marginal hands
Applying HU thin-value logic to 3-way rivers
coaching_context.population_note: Multiway river thin value thresholds are much higher than HU. Second pair vs two opponents who both checked Ax-heavy boards is a clear check — protect your showdown equity.
coaching_context.key_concept: Second pair on an A-high board in a 3-way pot is far too thin to bet.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Multiway River — Second Pair, Check or Thin Value? is difficult because many players default to thin-value betting in multiway river pots with marginal hands, even though T9s changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check Back works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: Multiway River — Top Pair, Small Value Bet in 3-Way
prompt: 3-way pot to river (BTN/MP/BB). Pot $90. Board K♣7♠2♦4♥J♣ (dry). MP and BB check. Hero (BTN) has K♥Q♦ (TPGK). Bet or check?

options.label: Check Back
Bet Small (~1/4-1/3 pot)
Bet Large (~2/3 pot)

answer.explanation: TPGK in multiway where both opponents checked every street is a small-bet spot. A 1/3-pot bet extracts thin value from Kx-worse, 7x, Jx. Large sizing is wrong — check-raises are more likely 3-way and large bets only get called by hands that beat or tie you.

A.explanation: Competent players check Kx sets and two-pair — TPGK is a marginal value bet in 3-way. Check is safer.

B.explanation: Passive recs check Kx-worse, 7x, Jx. Small bet extracts from the bottom of their range — don't go large.

C.explanation: Aggro pool checks down medium-strength hands too. Small bet acceptable; check also fine.

common_mistakes: Checking back TPGK in multiway when the runout is dry and both opponents checked
Betting large with TPGK in 3-way and getting raised off the best hand
coaching_context.population_note: Multiway TPGK river value requires small sizing discipline. The sweet spot is 1/3 pot — captures calls from worse Kx and pairs while minimizing exposure to check-raises.
coaching_context.key_concept: TPGK in multiway where both opponents checked every street is a small-bet spot. A shifts to check back when the pool tightens or widens enough to change the threshold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Multiway River — Top Pair, Small Value Bet in 3-Way is difficult because many players default to checking back TPGK in multiway when the runout is dry and both opponents checked, even though KQo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: A 1/3-pot bet extracts thin value from Kx-worse, 7x, and Jx in a 3-way checked-to pot — large sizing is wrong because check-raises are more likely multiway and your hand does not warrant building a big pot.

title: Multiway River — Missed Draw, Bluff or Give Up?
prompt: 3-way pot to river. Board 9♠8♦4♣3♠7♥. Two opponents remain. Hero has A♠K♠ (flopped nut flush draw, missed; turn gave OESD, missed). Both opponents check. Bluff or check?

options.label: Check Back (give up)
Bluff Small
Bluff Large

answer.explanation: Bluffing into two opponents on a 9-8-4-3-7 connected river is -EV regardless of pool. Both need to fold; live pools almost never fold multiway rivers with any pair. Check back and accept the loss — bluffing multiway with no equity is a pure spew.

A.explanation: Competent players check back strong hands in multiway — bluffing two opponents requires both to fold. Near-impossible on 9-8-4-3-7.

B.explanation: Passive recs call rivers with any pair. Zero fold equity vs two players — don't bluff.

C.explanation: Even aggro pools: bluffing two opponents simultaneously on a connected board is nearly impossible. Check.

common_mistakes: Bluffing missed draws into multiway pots on connected boards
Treating multiway bluffing like a HU opportunity
coaching_context.population_note: The fundamental multiway bluff rule: both opponents need to fold. On connected boards with live pools, this never happens. Take the free showdown — preserve stack discipline.
coaching_context.key_concept: Bluffing into two opponents on a 9-8-4-3-7 connected river is -EV regardless of pool.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Multiway River — Missed Draw, Bluff or Give Up? is difficult because many players default to bluffing missed draws into multiway pots on connected boards, even though AKs changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Check Back (give up) works because AKs lands on the right side of the value-versus-bluff threshold for the exact line described in the drill.
